The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. That balance protects legitimate discretion while keeping the responsible institution accountable.

PART 3 · 4

Should doping bans always be identical?

Suggested phrasal verbs
send a player off
Model answer · 128 words

No. Consistency requires comparable conduct to receive comparable treatment, not every case to receive one automatic penalty. Deliberate use, contamination, a valid therapeutic-use exemption and responsibility among athlete support staff may involve different circumstances. A proportional sanction should preserve deterrence while recognising evidence about fault where the rules permit it. Published reasoning and independent adjudication reduce arbitrary variation. The objective is doping-free sport achieved through a system athletes and the public can regard as principled. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. That balance protects legitimate discretion while keeping the responsible institution accountable.

PART 3 · 5

How should sport define eligibility categories?

Suggested phrasal verbs
call a decision back
Model answer · 119 words

An eligibility rule should identify a difference that is relevant to meaningful competition, such as age, weight or a functional effect in para-sport. Governing bodies should apply a classification-validity test, consult affected athletes and review evidence over time. Every classification boundary creates difficult edge cases, so transitional arrangements and appeal routes matter. The institution should explain the sporting purpose rather than present the category as self-evident. Transparent evidence cannot remove disagreement, but it makes exclusion more accountable. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy.

PART 3 · 6

Who should make return-to-play decisions?

Suggested phrasal verbs
check in with officials
Model answer · 120 words

Qualified clinicians with sufficient independence should make them through a published injury protocol. Coaches and athletes can provide information, but selection pressure gives them a short-term incentive to minimise symptoms. Formal return-to-play clearance should consider recovery, functional testing and the risk of recurrence. Governing bodies should also examine the longitudinal injury outcome, not only whether a player returned quickly. This structure protects athlete welfare while allowing the competitive staff to focus on performance once medical criteria are satisfied. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy.

PART 3 · 7

Does elite sport place athlete welfare at risk?

Suggested phrasal verbs
stand by a ruling
Model answer · 119 words

It can, because contracts, selection and identity reward continued performance even when rest is necessary. Dense calendars raise training load and overtraining risk, while athletes may fear reporting pain or abuse. athlete-centred governance should include independent medical care, a credible safeguarding-reporting pathway and athlete representation in schedule decisions. Elite sport need not be inherently harmful, but institutions must measure long-term physical and psychological outcomes instead of treating short careers and preventable injuries as unavoidable evidence of commitment. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy.

PART 3 · 8

Should governments fund grassroots or elite sport?

Suggested phrasal verbs
appeal against a sanction
Model answer · 118 words

Governments should protect grassroots participation first because local facilities, affordable sessions and trained volunteers benefit a much wider population. Elite programmes may create national moments and specialised knowledge, but their support should include public-funding conditionality on governance, safeguarding and community access. A transparent portfolio can fund both without assuming they produce identical value. A social-return estimate should include participation, health and belonging, while elite success remains one outcome rather than the sole justification for public spending. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy.

PART 3 · 9

How can clubs remove pay-to-play barriers?

Suggested phrasal verbs
test positive for
Model answer · 118 words

They should calculate the full participation cost, including the membership fee, equipment and travel, then target the mechanisms that exclude families. Concessions, an equipment loan, public transport support and genuinely free beginner session places can reduce a pay-to-play barrier. Application procedures should protect dignity and avoid excessive proof. Clubs also need suitable times and facilities. Tracking a participation-rate disparity by income or area can show whether formal assistance actually changes sustained attendance rather than registration alone. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy.

PART 3 · 10

Is early talent identification helpful?

Suggested phrasal verbs
clear an athlete to play
Model answer · 127 words

It can direct coaching and opportunity, but prediction in childhood is uncertain. talent identification often confuses early maturation, family resources and current training with long-term potential. A selection-bias audit can reveal whose ability is repeatedly overlooked. Systems should maintain a broad participation pathway, allow later entry and avoid making one early decision permanent. Identification is most defensible when it offers additional development without removing ordinary participation or education from children who are not selected. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. That balance protects legitimate discretion while keeping the responsible institution accountable.

PART 3 · 11

Does early specialisation harm children?

Suggested phrasal verbs
sit a match out
Model answer · 128 words

It can increase repetitive load, pressure and loss of enjoyment, especially when adults treat one sport as a child’s entire future. early specialisation may sometimes suit an older, well-informed athlete, but younger children generally benefit from varied movement and social experiences. A dual-career pathway, rest and meaningful choice protect development. Coaches should monitor injury and motivation rather than praising endurance of every demand. The aim is sustained capability, not the earliest possible adult-style training schedule. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. That balance protects legitimate discretion while keeping the responsible institution accountable.

PART 3 · 12

How do clubs create collective identity?

Suggested phrasal verbs
train up as a coach
Model answer · 128 words

They create it through repeated participation, shared memory and mutual obligation rather than branding alone. A community sports club becomes meaningful when players, supporters and volunteers maintain a local sporting tradition and help one another beyond a result. These relationships produce sporting social capital. Identity remains healthy when membership is open and disagreement is possible. If a club uses history to exclude newcomers, the same solidarity that connects insiders can damage its wider civic value. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. That balance protects legitimate discretion while keeping the responsible institution accountable.

PART 3 · 13

When does supporter culture become exclusionary?

Suggested phrasal verbs
turn spectators away
Model answer · 121 words

It becomes exclusionary when loyalty is defined through hostility, abuse or a claim that only one social group belongs. A clear sporting code of conduct, trained supporter liaison and consistent discipline should protect passionate expression while setting boundaries around harm. Clubs should consult minority supporters and examine reporting data. They need not make crowds silent or identical. The standard is whether supporter culture lets people cheer a team on without intimidation reducing the practical safety and membership of others. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy.

PART 3 · 14

Do mega-events create lasting social value?

Suggested phrasal verbs
cheer a team on
Model answer · 123 words

They can, but the outcome depends on planning before the bid and use after the event. New facilities may support participation, yet expensive specialist venues can remain inaccessible. An event-legacy evaluation should establish baseline use, identify intended groups and measure access several years later. Contracts can support local skills and transport, while community organisations need resources to use the infrastructure. A full social-return estimate should also record displacement, debt and opportunity cost rather than counting every visitor as a durable benefit. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy.

PART 3 · 15

What would a trustworthy sports system look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
rally around a club
Model answer · 124 words

It would combine competitive integrity, athlete welfare and open participation. Rules and selection standards would be public, officials trained, conflicts disclosed and serious decisions open to independent appeal. Medical and safeguarding staff would have authority separate from competitive pressure. Community facilities and affordable entry would widen the base, while public funds carried clear conditions. The system would measure both performance and a community-belonging measure, then correct disparities. Trust would come from consistent conduct and visible learning, not from demanding loyalty after failure. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy. The people affected should be consulted because implementation can differ from the written policy.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Video review should be used in all major sports. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
video-review thresholdclose callmatch officialofficiating consistencycompetitive integrityreviewable decisionappeal mechanismlevel playing fieldsporting merit
Model essay · 305 words

Video review promises more accurate decisions, but it also changes the rhythm and authority of a contest. Its advantages outweigh its disadvantages in sports where a narrow video-review threshold and rapid procedure can correct important errors. It should not be imposed identically on every incident or activity.

The strongest benefit is correction of a decisive close call that a match official could not observe clearly in real time. Shared images can improve officiating consistency and protect competitive integrity. A short explanation also helps players and spectators understand why an initial ruling changed. Published reasons would allow athletes and supporters to distinguish correction from arbitrary intervention. Consultation matters because the people affected often recognise practical barriers that a central body misses.

However, technology does not remove judgement. Officials still decide whether evidence is conclusive, and repeated interruptions can damage flow. Wealthy competitions may have tools unavailable elsewhere, while selective review can make similar incidents receive different treatment. A system that examines trivial contact but misses serious harm may appear precise without being fair. The outcome should be reviewed across groups rather than inferred from one prominent success. This safeguard connects a legitimate sporting objective with a procedure capable of earning trust.

Each sport should therefore define a limited set of reviewable decision categories, a time limit and final authority. Training and published examples can support consistency, while an appeal mechanism remains available for serious disciplinary matters that require fuller evidence. Reviews should be evaluated for accuracy, delay and unequal application. Independent scrutiny would also reduce conflicts between immediate competitive pressure and long-term public value.

In conclusion, video review creates net value when it corrects consequential errors through a transparent and restrained process. It should support human officiating rather than promise impossible perfection. Clear thresholds and accountability are more important than the mere presence of additional cameras.

TASK 2 · 2

Anti-doping rules should impose the same ban whenever an athlete fails a test. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Optional collocation bank
anti-doping regimeproportional sanctionstrict-liability principledoping-free sporttherapeutic-use exemptionathlete support staffsample-collection chainevidentiary standardathlete due process
Model essay · 312 words

Uniform bans appear to offer clarity and strong deterrence after a failed anti-doping test. However, I believe a credible anti-doping regime should combine firm responsibility with a proportional sanction because identical laboratory findings can arise through materially different conduct.

Supporters of one fixed ban argue that athletes must control what enters their bodies. The strict-liability principle reduces excuses, simplifies enforcement and protects doping-free sport. Variable penalties may also let famous competitors obtain favourable treatment through better legal representation. Published reasons would allow athletes and supporters to distinguish correction from arbitrary intervention. Consultation matters because the people affected often recognise practical barriers that a central body misses. The outcome should be reviewed across groups rather than inferred from one prominent success.

The opposing view is that deliberate enhancement, contaminated products and necessary treatment are not equally blameworthy. A valid therapeutic-use exemption or evidence about athlete support staff may alter responsibility. Punishment that ignores relevant facts can destroy a career without improving health or deterrence. The outcome should be reviewed across groups rather than inferred from one prominent success. This safeguard connects a legitimate sporting objective with a procedure capable of earning trust. Independent scrutiny would also reduce conflicts between immediate competitive pressure and long-term public value.

Rules should set clear sanction ranges and require a secure sample-collection chain, independent adjudication and an intelligible evidentiary standard. athlete due process must provide notice and access to evidence. Published decisions can expose inconsistency while preserving the capacity to distinguish fault. Independent scrutiny would also reduce conflicts between immediate competitive pressure and long-term public value. Published reasons would allow athletes and supporters to distinguish correction from arbitrary intervention.

In conclusion, strong anti-doping enforcement does not require an identical ban in every case. Consistency means treating comparable conduct alike through transparent criteria. Proportionate decisions can protect clean competition while maintaining the procedural legitimacy on which long-term compliance depends.

TASK 2 · 3

More children are specialising in one sport at an early age. What problems can this cause, and what measures could address them?

Optional collocation bank
early specialisationtraining loadovertraining risktalent identificationdual-career pathwayselection-bias auditvolunteer coachparticipation pathwaycompetitive integrity
Model essay · 308 words

Early concentration on one sport is often presented as the necessary route to elite success. Yet early specialisation can produce physical, educational and emotional problems when prediction is uncertain. Youth systems should preserve varied participation and introduce intensive training only with safeguards and meaningful child choice.

Repetitive movement and a high training load may increase injury and overtraining risk. A child whose identity depends on selection may hide pain or lose enjoyment. Time spent on travel and competition can also weaken education and friendships, leaving few options if performance stalls. Published reasons would allow athletes and supporters to distinguish correction from arbitrary intervention. Consultation matters because the people affected often recognise practical barriers that a central body misses. The outcome should be reviewed across groups rather than inferred from one prominent success.

Selection itself can be distorted. talent identification may reward early maturity, family income and prior coaching rather than long-term potential. Children excluded early lose access, while selected athletes may interpret one decision as a permanent verdict about their future. The outcome should be reviewed across groups rather than inferred from one prominent success. This safeguard connects a legitimate sporting objective with a procedure capable of earning trust.

Clubs should offer multi-sport foundations, planned rest and a dual-career pathway. A selection-bias audit can test who enters and leaves development programmes. Independent medical staff, trained parents and each volunteer coach should monitor wellbeing, while late-entry routes keep the participation pathway open. Independent scrutiny would also reduce conflicts between immediate competitive pressure and long-term public value. Published reasons would allow athletes and supporters to distinguish correction from arbitrary intervention.

In conclusion, early specialisation can narrow health, identity and opportunity before children can evaluate the trade-off. Broader movement, transparent selection and protected education would allow serious talent to develop without treating every young participant as material for an elite system.

TASK 2 · 4

Local sports clubs are among the best ways to strengthen communities. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
community sports clubsporting social capitallocal sporting traditioncommunity pitchmembership feesupporter culturesporting code of conductcommunity-belonging measurecompetitive integrity
Model essay · 311 words

Local sports clubs can create repeated contact, shared routines and practical cooperation. I largely agree that a well-run community sports club is a powerful community institution, although its social value depends on access, safeguarding and conduct rather than sport automatically producing cohesion.

Regular participation builds relationships across seasons. Players, parents, officials and volunteers exchange help and develop sporting social capital. A local sporting tradition can connect generations, while a familiar clubhouse or community pitch gives residents a place to gather around a shared activity. Published reasons would allow athletes and supporters to distinguish correction from arbitrary intervention. Consultation matters because the people affected often recognise practical barriers that a central body misses. The outcome should be reviewed across groups rather than inferred from one prominent success.

However, clubs can reproduce exclusion. A high membership fee, inaccessible facility or hostile supporter culture may restrict who belongs. Strong internal loyalty can also become abuse of opponents or newcomers. Social connection among insiders is not sufficient evidence of broad public value. The outcome should be reviewed across groups rather than inferred from one prominent success. This safeguard connects a legitimate sporting objective with a procedure capable of earning trust.

Public support should therefore require a clear sporting code of conduct, safeguarding, affordable beginner places and accessible equipment. A community-belonging measure should include new and minority participants, not only long-term members. Local partnerships can connect the club with schools, health services and residents who do not yet participate. Independent scrutiny would also reduce conflicts between immediate competitive pressure and long-term public value. Published reasons would allow athletes and supporters to distinguish correction from arbitrary intervention.

In conclusion, sports clubs can strengthen communities because they organise sustained shared action, not because competition is inherently inclusive. When barriers and harmful conduct are addressed, a club can become civic infrastructure whose relationships extend well beyond the final score.

TASK 2 · 5

Hosting a major international sporting event creates more problems than benefits for a city. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Optional collocation bank
event-legacy evaluationgrassroots participationcollective identityfacility access gappublic-funding conditionalitysocial-return estimatecompetitive integritylevel playing fieldsporting merit
Model essay · 302 words

Major sporting events offer spectacle, visitors and a deadline for investment, but they can also create debt and underused infrastructure. I believe benefits can exceed problems only where a city starts with a credible long-term plan and subjects claims to an independent event-legacy evaluation.

Supporters argue that an event can accelerate transport improvements, train workers and create a shared civic moment. Reusable venues may widen grassroots participation, and international attention can support tourism. Elite competition can also strengthen collective identity when residents see themselves represented without hostility. Published reasons would allow athletes and supporters to distinguish correction from arbitrary intervention. Consultation matters because the people affected often recognise practical barriers that a central body misses.

Critics note that bidding costs, security and specialist construction can divert money from ordinary services. Residents may face displacement while a new stadium does little to close the facility access gap. Visitor spending is often counted without considering imports, public subsidy or what the same budget could have achieved elsewhere. The outcome should be reviewed across groups rather than inferred from one prominent success. This safeguard connects a legitimate sporting objective with a procedure capable of earning trust.

Hosts should publish contracts, protect housing and design venues around post-event users before construction. public-funding conditionality can require accessibility, community booking and transparent maintenance costs. A social-return estimate should compare outcomes with a realistic alternative and continue for several years. Independent scrutiny would also reduce conflicts between immediate competitive pressure and long-term public value. Published reasons would allow athletes and supporters to distinguish correction from arbitrary intervention.

In conclusion, a mega-event is neither a guaranteed catalyst nor an unavoidable waste. Its value depends on governance, opportunity cost and durable public use. Cities should host only when the event serves an existing development strategy rather than becoming the strategy itself.

Course index · Module 1 · Chapter 23

Academic English Studio · Topic 23 · Sport, Fairness and Collective Identity
